profile_1005_gemma3:27b.thinking_process: Look, I’m a mechanic. I fix cars, alright? Not a philosopher. But I talk to people all day, every day. You gotta judge how likely something is – will a customer pay on time? Will this engine give me trouble? Will Racing Club *finally* win a championship? You get a feel for it. These phrases… they aren't numbers, not really. They're about *feeling* confident, or not. “Seguro” means you’d bet your last peso on it. “Quizás”... well, that's hoping for the best while bracing for the worst. “Poco probable” means it's a long shot, a real *garron*. Sometimes I think people use them to be polite, you know? To soften bad news. Or to avoid committing to anything. I mostly use “creo que sí” and “puede ser”. Keeps things easy. When I really *know* something, I say “seguro”. If I haven't a clue, “no sé” is honest enough. When combining things, it gets tricky. If two people are unsure, it’s still unsure. If one's certain… well, you have to weigh that. It's all just a guess, really, a feeling in the gut. I try to be realistic, though. Life’s hard enough without chasing dreams that’ll never come true.

profile_1006_gemma3:27b.thinking_process: Ay, this is interesting. I sell cookware, you know? Mostly to families, women mostly. I use "Quizás" and “Puede ser” a lot when I’m unsure about a delivery date or if something's in stock. It’s polite, you know? Keeps them from getting angry if it doesn't work out. “Seguro” is strong, I only use that when I’m positive – like, if I *know* the warranty covers something. “Lo dudo” is the opposite, like, “I doubt it,” when a customer asks for a discount I can’t give. It’s all about being careful with your words, especially when you're trying to make a sale. People get easily upset. When someone is *really* unsure, they say “No sé.” It’s honest, I guess. Combining what two people say… hmm, that's tricky. If one person is certain and the other isn't, you have to lean towards the certain one, but not entirely ignore the other. It’s like negotiating, finding the middle ground. I always try to be optimistic, though. It helps with sales.

Look, I sell insurance, right? So I deal with probabilities *all* the time. People don't think about it, but when they buy a policy, they're basically deciding how likely something bad is to happen. I don't get into fancy maths, though. It's all gut feeling, what sounds right when I'm talking to a client. "Probable" is good for encouraging someone to protect themselves, while "improbable" is…well, you try to be honest, but you also have a job to do.

With phrases, it depends on how I’m saying it, the tone, you know? “Creo que sí” – that means I’m leaning towards yes, but I’m not betting my life on it. “Poco probable” is pretty clear, though. And “No sé”… that's when I really need to understand the risk better. People here, we're generally pretty direct. We don't beat around the bush with percentages, unless maybe it’s someone technical. Mostly, it’s about feeling confident in what you’re saying, building trust. Combining opinions? That’s trickier. If two people both say something is likely, that’s pretty strong. If one’s unsure and the other says yes… well, you need to figure out *why* they believe that. I have to read people, understand what's behind the words. It's all about the nuances, not just the words themselves.

profile_1044_gemma3:27b.thinking_process:
Look, I'm a simple man. I work in an office, pushing papers mostly. Not much call for philosophical debates about *probability*, you know? But we all use these expressions all the time, just chatting. "Creo que sí" – that means I think it’s likely, but I wouldn’t bet my paycheck on it. “Poco probable” is when something seems a bit far-fetched, like my cousin becoming a famous tango dancer. “Seguro”... that’s rare. You really need to be certain before saying that. Usually, things are somewhere in between.

When people ask about something, I try to be honest. "Quizás" is my go-to when I genuinely haven't got a clue. "Puede ser" is similar, more like saying “it's *possible*”. When giving a range, I'm thinking about how much wiggle room I need. Is it *really* unlikely, or just not super likely? I tend to be a bit cautious, if I'm honest. I don’t like making promises I can't keep.

Combining opinions? That’s tricky. If two people both say "quizás," it doesn’t really change much, does it? Still just a guess. If someone gives a number, like 80%, I tend to take that at face value. If one person is certain and the other is just saying “surely,” I’ll lean towards the certainty. It's just how things are done, you try to find the middle ground. Most of the time, you just hope you’re not wrong.

Ay, Dios mío, this is a bit complicated, isn’t it? I didn't get to finish school, you see. My husband, Ricardo, he worked construction, a good man, gone now, bless his soul. I did paperwork at the local bakery for years, mostly taking orders, keeping track of the daily bread. Numbers aren’t really my strong suit. But I understand people, and how they *say* things. 

When someone says "creo que sí," it's not a guarantee, more like... they think so, but could be wrong. "Improbable" means you really don’t think it will happen, close to impossible. “Probable” is… well, it *could* happen, you’d say that if you didn’t want to commit either way. It's how people talk, you know? They don't always say exactly what they mean.

"Seguro" is solid, you bet on it. "Quizás" is a maybe, a real maybe. "Puede ser," same thing. I use “puede ser” all the time when my grandkids ask if they can have another *factura* – “puede ser,” meaning maybe if you behave! 

When people give two opinions, you have to think… how sure are both of them? If both say “quizás,” it’s still just a maybe, isn’t it? If one is sure and the other isn’t, you have to lean towards the more certain one. It's all about figuring out how much weight to give each person's words. It’s like when the ladies at the bakery would gossip – you had to decide who was exaggerating and who was telling it straight. I’ve had a lot of practice figuring things out, even without a fancy education.

When people try to put numbers on it, it gets confusing. I use “quizás” and “puede ser” almost the same way, it’s just a maybe, a possibility. Sometimes a small one, sometimes… well, maybe a little better. When I hear two people giving opinions, I try to see who's the more reliable one, who has more experience with whatever they're talking about. Then I lean a little more towards their side. If they both say almost the same thing, then I figure they’re probably right. If one says "no way" and the other says “sure,” I get very confused and try to stay out of the conversation. Honestly, it’s usually better to just avoid making predictions altogether. Too much can happen in a day.

profile_1134_gemma3:27b.thinking_process:
Look, I work in an office, mostly dealing with paperwork and some basic accounting. Not exactly rocket science, but it pays the bills, you know? We talk about possibilities all day long – will a payment come through, will a client approve something, will the boss be in a good mood. You get used to softening things, not being too direct. “Maybe” and “probably” are your friends. Saying something is *certain* is rare, unless it’s about the sun rising. Even then, you don’t want to jinx it.

I think about how I *feel* when I say these things. “Seguro” – that’s strong. You really have to believe it. “Poco probable” is a polite way of saying “it’s not going to happen”, especially to a client. "Quizás" is like a hedge, giving yourself an out. If it happens, great. If it doesn't, you never really said it *would*.  

It's not about exact numbers, really. It's about how much weight you give something. A lot of it depends on who you’re talking to, too. You wouldn't tell your boss "Lo dudo" even if you thought something was hopeless. You’d say “Es complicado” or something like that.  I don't sit and think “Oh, that’s 65% likely”. It’s just… a feeling.  But I can try to give numbers if pushed, it just feels a bit artificial. Combining opinions… that’s even harder. You’re trying to guess what *they* meant, then put it together with what *you* meant. It’s a bit of a mess, honestly. I try to be reasonable, to not go to extremes.

"Seguro" is rare, I tend not to be *completely* certain of anything. And “difícilmente”... that suggests a real struggle against the odds. 

When people give different opinions, well, you have to try and find a middle ground. It depends on *who* is saying it, of course. profile_1245_gemma3:27b.thinking_process: Look, I spend my days convincing people to invest in things – real estate mostly, sometimes land. It's all about perception, isn't it? And a big part of that is how you talk about risk. You can't say something is *certain* to succeed, because that's just… naive. Everything has a chance of failing. But you can say it's *probable*, or *likely*, and downplay the 'improbables'. It's a dance. I’m a fairly direct guy, though. I don't really bother with overly flowery language. "Creo que sí" is just a polite way of saying you’re leaning towards yes, and "pienso que sí" is almost the same. ‘Quizás’ and ‘puede ser’… well, those are hedging your bets. They mean you haven’t really made up your mind. When I say “seguro,” I mean I’ve done my homework and feel confident. Not absolute, mind you, but confident enough to put my reputation on the line. I find Argentines tend to use these expressions more casually than, say, Americans. We're comfortable with ambiguity. It's part of our culture. Things rarely go exactly according to plan, so admitting uncertainty feels… natural. Combining them is trickier. If someone says "quizás" and another says "quizás," you’re still pretty much in the dark. But if one person is more certain, you have to factor that in. It’s about weighting the information, really. I’m good at weighting information. It’s my job.

profile_1246_gemma3:27b.thinking_process: Look, I’ve lived a long life, worked hard, seen a lot of things happen and a lot *not* happen. When someone asks me if something’s going to happen, I don’t think in numbers. I think in feelings, in how things usually go. “Seguro” – that’s solid, you can bet on it. “Imposible” is almost the same, though nothing is truly impossible, God willing. "Probable" just means it seems likely, like the sun rising tomorrow. But things change. Things *always* change. "Quizás"… that’s the most honest word, really. A bit maybe, a bit not. Life is full of “quizás”. I use “creo que sí” a lot, mostly to be polite, even if I’m not completely convinced. I try to avoid saying things are certain, too much pride goes before a fall, you know? If someone asks me something complicated, I usually say “puede ser”, a way of saying “I haven’t thought about it much” or “it’s not my place to judge”. With numbers, I get lost. A percentage? Bah. It's all just talk to make people feel better or worse. It's about how you *feel* about something, not how likely it is according to some calculation. My old man always said, "Trust your gut, son." And that's what I do.

profile_1247_gemma3:27b.thinking_process: Look, I deal with people all day, right? Selling things. You learn to read how likely someone is to buy, to say “yes”. It’s all about feeling it out, and using the right words to nudge them. *Creo que sí* and *pienso que sí*… those are like, “I think so, leaning towards yes”. Not a guarantee, but I'm hopeful. *Probable* is a solid bet, something with a good chance. *Poco probable* is when I feel resistance, but there’s still a tiny spark. *Improbable*? That's usually a lost cause, but I still try to be polite. *Muy improbable* is almost the same, but stronger. *Casi seguro* means I can practically count on it, just needs a little push. *Quizás* and *puede ser* are the tricky ones. Maybe, maybe not. I use them when I don't want to commit, or when it really could go either way. *Seguramente* is like, 80% sure. *Muy probable* is like 70-75%. *Igual al azar* means a complete toss-up, like flipping a coin. *No sé* is what it sounds like. Honestly, I try not to use that too much at work. *Lo dudo* is a polite way of saying "I don't think so". *Seguro* is…well, sure. Certain. *Difícilmente* is close to impossible. When combining things, you have to consider where both people are coming from. If someone's already leaning towards "no" and the other says "maybe"... that's still leaning towards "no", you know? I try to find the middle ground, a realistic estimate. It's about being practical, really. I'm a salesperson, not a philosopher.

profile_1248_gemma3:27b.thinking_process:
Look, I’ve spent my life selling things – mostly household goods, you know, pots, pans, the basics. You learn to read people, and part of that is reading *how* they say things. Not just what they say, but the little words they use to soften a refusal or hint at a maybe. "Perhaps" isn't the same as "probably," not by a long shot. I always paid attention to that. A “maybe” meant I needed to push a little more, offer a small discount. A “probably not” was usually a firm no.

These words about probability… they’re just how we hedge our bets, aren’t they? We don’t want to be held to a hard promise, or look foolish if we're wrong. “Perhaps” is polite, keeps the door open. “Definitely” is strong, commits you. "I doubt it" is a softer "no" than a straight "impossible."

When people are giving probabilities, it's even trickier. Numbers feel… cold.  I prefer the feel of the language.  If someone says “very likely,” I’m thinking 80, 90 percent.  But if they give me a number, I always take it with a grain of salt.  People round things up, they exaggerate.  And if they give two opinions? Well, you have to find the middle ground, right?  An average is the only sensible thing. It's about being realistic. Life isn’t black and white, it's a lot of greys.

profile_1300_gemma3:27b.thinking_process: Look, I finished school, right? Not a university degree, but enough to get by. I work in logistics, coordinating shipments for a company that exports wine. It's decent money, enough for a comfortable life, a small apartment in Ramos Mejía, and maybe a trip to Mar del Plata every year. People around here – my friends, my family – we're pretty straightforward. We say what we mean. When it comes to chances, well, it depends. "Seguro" means you *know* it's happening. "Probable" means it's likely, you'd bet on it. "Poco probable"… that's a long shot. I use "quizás" and "puede ser" all the time, mostly when I don’t want to commit to an answer, or when I'm being polite. It's not that I'm trying to be evasive, it’s just… life is uncertain, you know? Things change. I wouldn’t say something is "seguro" unless I *really* mean it. It feels arrogant, almost. Combining opinions? That's tougher. It’s like trying to average feelings. You have to think about who’s saying it, their usual way of speaking. If someone’s always pessimistic, you discount their opinion a little. If someone's a dreamer, you take it with a grain of salt. I generally try to be realistic, so I’ll usually land somewhere in the middle. I try not to be too optimistic, or too negative. It doesn't help anyone.

"Quizás" doesn't mean the same thing every time. It depends on *who* says it, and *how*. A customer saying "Quizás" means they’re politely telling me no, probably. "Poco probable" leaves a little room, a little hope. I use "Creo que sí" and "Pienso que sí" almost interchangeably, unless I’m really trying to be forceful, then I'd just say "Sí." Combining things...that's trickier. If two people are unsure, it doesn’t suddenly become more certain, does it? You have to weigh who’s saying it, their position. It’s all gut feeling, really. profile_1303_gemma3:27b.thinking_process:
Ay, Dios mío, this is a bit of a head-scratcher, isn’t it? All this talk about probability… we don’t really *think* like that around here, you know? We just *feel* it. If something feels likely, we say so. If it feels… not so much, we say that. “Probable” and “improbable” are things you hear on the radio, on the news, not really how we talk amongst ourselves. I finished school, but it wasn't university, just secondary. My husband, Ricardo, he worked construction all his life, a good, honest man. We’ve always just gotten by. We don't deal in percentages.

“Creo que sí” and “Pienso que sí”... those are almost the same, like saying “I reckon so”. It’s not a guarantee, but it’s leaning that way. “Poco probable” and “muy improbable” are easy enough - things that aren't happening. “Casi seguro” is when you’re practically counting on it, almost like you’ve already seen it happen in your head. “Quizás” and “puede ser” are just… maybe. It could go either way. 

When you put them together, well, that's where it gets tricky. If two people both say “quizás”, it's still just a maybe, isn't it? If someone says they're sure and another says they're not… you have to lean towards the sure one, I suppose. It’s all just common sense, really. I try to be honest, and I trust my gut. Numbers… well, numbers are for accountants.

profile_1306_gemma3:27b.thinking_process:
Look, I sell things, right? Mostly construction materials. I’m talking to contractors, homeowners, foremen… all day, every day. You learn to read people, and you learn to talk in a way that doesn’t scare them off or get them thinking you’re trying to rip them off. Certainty doesn’t sell. A little doubt… a little wiggle room… that gives them space to make a decision. "Maybe this, maybe that," that’s how you guide them. I’m not a philosopher. I just know what works.

These expressions… they’re all about softening things. “Seguro” is pretty direct, of course, but even then, you don't use it too often. It sounds like you're guaranteeing something, and that’s trouble. "Creo que sí" is a good one. It's positive, but it leaves room for things to change. "Quizás" and “Puede ser”… those are your go-to’s when you’re not sure and don’t want to commit. “Improbable” and “Muy improbable” are useful if someone’s asking about something really far-fetched, like ordering a special tile that takes months to arrive. "Lo dudo" is just a polite way of saying "no," usually. If someone asks if we have something in stock we definitely don't, I’ll say “Lo dudo,” rather than a flat "No." It’s about being friendly, you know?

Combining things… that's tricky. If two people both say "Quizás," it still means "Quizás," doesn't it? If someone says 80% and another says 72%, I'm going to lean towards the lower end, because people always exaggerate. I’m a practical guy. I don’t overthink it.

profile_1318_gemma3:27b.thinking_process:
Honestly, this is a bit academic for a Tuesday, but alright. I suppose thinking about how we *say* things is useful, even if it feels a bit like dissecting a butterfly. We Argentines, we’re… expressive. We use a lot of nuance. A simple ‘yes’ or ‘no’ feels almost rude sometimes. So, these probability expressions, they aren't just about the chance of something happening; they’re about politeness, about softening a blow, or about showing you’ve considered things.

‘Seguro’ and ‘Casi seguro’ are pretty straightforward – those are high probabilities, maybe 90-100%. ‘Improbable’ and ‘Muy improbable’ are equally clear, close to zero. The trouble is with the middle ground. ‘Quizás’ and ‘Puede ser’... those are vague. They could mean 50/50, or they could be a way of saying “I don’t want to commit.” I use ‘Creo que sí’ and ‘Pienso que sí’ a lot – they’re ways to offer a positive outlook without guaranteeing anything. I’d put those around 70-80% usually, depending on the context and who I’m talking to. ‘Igual al azar’ is, well, random, so about 50%. ‘Lo dudo’ is a polite way of saying “I don’t believe you,” so lower than that, maybe 20-30%. ‘Difícilmente’ is similar, maybe even a little stronger.  I find myself saying 'No sé' more and more these days. It's the truth, often.

When combining probabilities, it gets tricky. If two people both say 'Quizás,' I don’t really take that as stronger evidence. It just means two people are being noncommittal. If someone gives a number, like 80%, it feels more definitive, though I’d generally average it a bit. If someone is *sure* – well, that’s pretty convincing, even if it clashes with another opinion. I consider the source too, of course.

These things… these probabilities… it feels a bit strange to put a number on it. Life isn't numbers. It's feelings, it's what you've seen. But I get what they want. I’ll think about what feels right, what *I* would say if someone asked me. 

“Quizás” and “Puede ser” are the same to me, more or less. A maybe. “Muy probable”... that’s pretty close to sure, but not quite. "Igual al azar" is like flipping a coin. You really have no idea. "Lo dudo" is like, you got a bad feeling. It’s hard when they ask you to combine things. If two people both say "Quizás," it still feels like a maybe, but maybe a little stronger. Look, I didn't finish university, alright? I went to a technical school, learned a trade – I’m a mechanic. I fix cars, not split hairs over what “probably” *really* means. "Probable" is when I've seen the problem before, I know roughly what’s wrong. "Poco probable" is close to "improbable" but maybe, *maybe* there's a small chance. 

I use "quizás" and "puede ser" almost interchangeably – meaning maybe, it could happen. “Seguramente” and "seguro" are pretty much the same, like I'm confident about something. "Muy probable" is like, 8 or 9 out of 10.  When I say “lo dudo” it means I really don’t think it’s going to work, that part is broken for good. "Difícilmente" is like a really tough repair – it *might* be possible, but it’ll take a lot of work and money. And "igual al azar" is just… who knows? Like flipping a coin. “No sé” is self-explanatory, right?

When people give me two opinions, I usually just average them out in my head. If someone says 80% and another says 72%, I figure it's around 75-ish. It's just a feeling, honestly. If someone is completely unsure ("no sé") and another has an idea (“creo que sí”), I lean towards the idea, because at least *someone* has a thought on it. It's all about getting a sense of things, you know? Not about perfect numbers.

“Probable” and “improbable” – those are okay for talking about general things, like if River’s going to win the next match. But for a specific job, you're more careful. “Seguro” is strong. Really strong. You only use that if you’re absolutely certain, because if you’re wrong, it’s on you. "Quizás" is useful when you really don't have a clue. It's honest, you know? "Poco probable" and “muy improbable”... those are mostly when things are clearly going to fail, but you try to be polite about it. 

When people give you two opinions, you kinda average them out in your head. If someone is really sure and someone else isn't, you lean a bit towards the sure one, but not all the way. It’s just… common sense. You listen to what they say, try to get a feel for how confident they are, and then you make your own judgment. It's about being realistic. People are always hoping for the best, but you need to be prepared for the worst.

And "difícilmente"? That’s basically a no. 

Combining things… that's harder. If two people both say "quizás," it doesn't really *add* anything, does it? It just confirms everyone's unsure. Numbers are tricky too. 80% and 72%… they’re both pretty high, so it's probably happening. But you gotta remember, people round up, they exaggerate. It’s just how it is. I try not to think too much about probabilities, to be honest. It's more about feeling the situation out.

We didn't talk about *probabilities* back then. We just *knew* things, or we didn’t. Or we guessed! Mostly we guessed. 

If I say “Creo que sí,” well, I believe it, but it's not like the sun’s going to rise tomorrow for certain. Things can change. “Improbable” just means it won’t happen, I wouldn’t count on it. "Seguro"… that's when my grandson finds the last alfajor. *That* is sure.

When people ask about things being likely, I just think about how often it happens. "Quizás" is just… well, maybe. Fifty-fifty, like a coin flip. “Puede ser” is like a softer “quizás”, a little less certain. When I combine things, it depends. If two people say “maybe,” it’s still just maybe. But if someone says "sure" and someone else says something like "maybe", I go with the sure one, naturally. It's just common sense, really. I don't think too much about numbers, though. It’s all feeling, mostly.

Nobody is ever *completely* sure. Even with the best guarantees, things fall through. I use "creo que sí" all the time – it's polite, it acknowledges possibility, but it’s generally optimistic. I’d say “poco probable” is much closer to nothing happening than it is to something happening, though. When I say "lo dudo," it's almost a polite way of saying “no,” really. When asked for a range, it depends on the context, doesn't it? Sometimes "quizás" could be almost nothing, other times nearly fifty-fifty. Combining things...that's trickier. It's about how much you trust the other person. If two people both say something is likely, you tend to believe it more. But if one is uncertain, it muddies the waters. I always try to be cautious, of course. A good clerical worker is cautious.

profile_1600_gemma3:27b.thinking_process:
Honestly, this is…interesting. People ask about probabilities all the time, but rarely in such a formal way. Usually, it’s just part of conversation. “Do you think River will win?” “Probably.” “Will it rain tomorrow?” “Perhaps.” It’s more about politeness, gauging the other person, than actual calculations. I work in an office, managing accounts – things need to be *fairly* precise there, but even then, we use these softening phrases. “We *expect* payment next week” isn’t the same as “Payment *will* be received next week,” you see? It leaves room for things to go wrong without being directly negative.

I think ‘Seguro’ and ‘Muy seguro’ are near enough the same, truly certain. ‘Quizás’ and ‘Puede ser’…similar too, a genuine possibility but not strong. ‘Improbable’ and ‘Muy improbable’ – a bit more of a difference there, the ‘muy’ really adds weight to the doubt. “Poco probable” I’d use if something is unlikely but *could* still happen; there’s a small chance. “Lo dudo” is more personal – it’s *my* doubt, not necessarily a statement of fact. 'Igual al azar' implies a true coin flip. 

When people give different answers, it’s about averaging them out, finding a middle ground. It depends on who is saying it too – my boss saying “Perhaps” means something different than my nephew saying “Perhaps”. But this survey asks for my *personal* interpretation, so that’s what I’ll focus on. I'll try to be consistent, as much as possible. It's all just feeling, really.

When people give you two opinions… that’s trickier. You have to weigh them, see which one seems more reasonable. If someone’s very sure, and someone else is just guessing, you lean towards the sure one, naturally. It’s all about judgment, really. It's about understanding what's being said, and what's *not* being said.

I've noticed people use "creo que sí" and "pienso que sí" pretty much the same way, almost interchangeably. They're not *certain*, just leaning that way. "Poco probable" is stronger than "quizás," definitely. And "difícilmente" is almost as bad as "muy improbable," but it feels more… final. Like, it’s really not going to happen.

When I’m talking to someone, I try to be clear. I don’t want them to misunderstand the chances. But sometimes you gotta read between the lines. If someone says "puede ser," they're probably more doubtful than they're letting on. Especially if they don’t look you in the eye when they say it. When combining probabilities… that's harder. It depends on who's saying it, how much I trust them. If two people both say something is likely, I’d bump up the number, obviously. But if one is uncertain and the other is hopeful, I'd probably land somewhere in the middle. It’s all about judgment, really.

profile_1652_gemma3:27b.thinking_process: Look, I didn't finish school, alright? Not like some people. I finished *secundaria*, that's it. I work cleaning houses, sometimes helping in a little *kiosk* when the owner needs it. So I'm not a scholar or anything. But I understand people, and I hear how they talk. When someone says "creo que sí", it just means they're leaning towards yes, but they aren’t totally convinced. It's not a strong yes. "Improbable"… well, that’s pretty clear, isn't it? Something's not likely to happen. I use "quizás" and "puede ser" a lot, to be polite, to not say no directly. It’s easier that way. "Seguro" is… well, sure. No doubt. I'd bet on it. “Lo dudo” means I don't believe it, plain and simple. Combining things… that's tricky. If two people both say "quizás," it doesn't make it more certain, does it? It just means nobody really knows. If someone says it's likely and someone says a number, I'd probably go with the number, if it’s high enough. People can be wishful thinkers, you know? I try to be realistic, that’s what my mother always told me. Life is hard enough without fooling yourself. It's all just trying to guess what’s going to happen next, isn’t it? And honestly, most of the time, you can't really know.

profile_1653_gemma3:27b.thinking_process:
Look, honestly, this is a bit weird. But okay, fine. I finished school, *secundaria*, and then…well, I haven’t really needed to *work*. My parents, they’re comfortable. We’re not super rich, but we live well in Belgrano. My friends are mostly the same. We talk about things, you know? Football, girls, what we’re going to do with our lives…even though most of us don’t have to worry about a job right now.

When we talk about whether something *will* happen, it’s never precise. It’s always…feeling. “Creo que sí” just means I lean that way. “Improbable” is, well, obvious. It’s just how we express things. "Quizás" and "Puede ser" are almost the same, right? Like, a general maybe. "Seguro" is strong, really confident. And when we're unsure, we just say "No sé." We don't think about numbers. It's all gut feeling, a sense of how likely something is to happen. 

Combining things… that’s trickier. If two people both say “Quizás,” it doesn’t suddenly become *more* certain, does it? It just confirms that no one really knows. If someone says something is 80% likely and another says 72%, I'd probably just average it out, roughly. It's not a science, though. It's about taking both opinions into account and getting a general feel. People here like to be polite, too. So someone saying "Seguramente" is pretty firm, even if it isn't *completely* certain.

I sell linens, you see. So, I'm always trying to read customers. *Probable* they’ll buy if they’re touching everything and asking about prices. *Improbable* if they just wander through. It’s all about reading people, really. This 'quizás' and 'puede ser' stuff… that's when I’m not sure *why* someone might buy, maybe they're saving up. “Lo dudo” is when I can just tell they're window shopping. And “igual al azar”... well, that’s like the lottery, isn’t it? Pure luck.

Combining probabilities… that's tougher. If two people both say 'quizás,' it doesn’t really add anything, does it? It's still just maybe. Numbers are easier, *más directo*. But even then, you have to consider who’s saying it. My *abuela* and I wouldn’t interpret the same percentage the same way. I try to be realistic, you know? I’m not a dreamer. Just a working woman trying to get by.

profile_1665_gemma3:27b.thinking_process: Look, I sell insurance, right? So I deal with probabilities *all* the time. People don't really think in exact percentages, not when they're talking about whether something *might* happen. They use feelings, you know? "Probable" isn't 70%, it's… well, it feels like it's likely to occur. I think a lot about risk and how to explain it to clients, and that definitely shapes how I understand these phrases. "Seguro" is almost never *actually* sure, it’s just very, very likely based on everything we know. "Quizás" and "puede ser" are pretty much the same to me – a shrug, maybe a 50/50 chance, or maybe leaning one way or the other depending on the context. I use "creo que sí" a lot when I’m trying to be positive, even if I’m not completely convinced myself. My grandmother used to say "difícilmente" about everything bad that *could* happen, and it always felt like almost impossible, but still… possible. The really tricky ones are when you try to put them together. You have to think about who said what and if they’re trying to be polite or realistic. It's about reading between the lines as much as understanding the words themselves. Honestly, I use these expressions more to soften things, to be polite, than to give a precise prediction. It’s important to be agreeable, especially when you’re trying to make a sale.

profile_1670_gemma3:27b.thinking_process: Look, I sell insurance, right? So I spend all day talking about *if* things happen, *how likely* they are. It's how I earn my living. People don't want to hear "maybe," they want a clear idea of risk. "Seguro" is obvious – 100 percent. "Imposible" would be zero, but we don't really use that much, do we? It's all shades of grey. I use “creo que sí” and “pienso que sí” all the time. When my daughter asks if I’ll make her empanadas, I always say “creo que sí.” If someone asks if it'll rain, I’ll say "quizás" or "puede ser." Look, I work in an office, mostly dealing with paperwork and coordinating things for the managers. It's not exactly rocket science, but you learn to be careful with how you say things. I use "creo que sí" and "pienso que sí" all the time – it’s polite, it shows you've thought about it, but doesn't commit you fully. “Probable” is good for when something feels likely, but still has a chance of falling through. “Improbable” or “muy improbable” are for things you strongly doubt, but you don’t want to be *completely* dismissive. It’s all about softening the edges, being diplomatic.

“Quizás” and “puede ser” are just…well, when you really haven’t got a clue. Honestly, sometimes you say them just to fill the silence. "Seguro" is strong; you have to be pretty confident before using that one. “Lo dudo” feels a bit more direct, a bit less polite than “poco probable”, you use it when you really think something won’t happen.

When people give probabilities, I don’t really focus on the exact number. I look at the phrasing, the way they say it. Two “quizás” answers…that’s just acknowledging you really have no idea. Two high numbers? You’d average them out, but you'd also think about *who* gave those numbers. Is one person always optimistic? Then you'd adjust downwards. It's about reading between the lines, really. It's how things get done here.

profile_1707_gemma3:27b.thinking_process: Okay, so this is about how likely things are, right? It's funny, because sometimes you *know* something will happen, and other times… well, in Buenos Aires, plans change constantly. You learn to be careful with how you say things. Like, "creo que sí" – that’s just being polite, hoping for the best, but not guaranteeing anything. “Seguro” is strong, you don’t say that lightly. "Improbable" and "muy improbable" are pretty straightforward, though. We use “quizás” and “puede ser” a lot, especially when someone asks if a football match will be good – you don't want to jinx it! “Lo dudo” is for when you *really* don't believe something, like when my cousin says he’s going to start going to the gym. With percentages, people get a bit… dramatic. They want to be definite. It’s like, if someone says 80%, they’re pretty convinced. When they give two opinions, you have to consider who's saying it. My *abuela* always thinks everything is a miracle, while my friend Mateo is a total pessimist. Combining them would be... tricky. I work in a shop selling leather goods, and I’m always estimating if someone will actually buy something. You get a feel for it, judging their body language and how much they’re browsing. It’s all about reading people, and knowing how they use words.

profile_1712_gemma3:27b.thinking_process: Look, I deal with people all day, convincing them things, negotiating prices…it’s all about how you *present* possibility. I use “quizás” and “puede ser” a lot when I'm feeling out a client, trying to gauge their interest without committing. It gives them room to maneuver. Honestly, sometimes “no sé” is the smartest answer, especially when things are volatile like they have been lately. Uncertainty is part of life, especially in sales. When I’m thinking about a deal, I’m always weighing possibilities, mentally calculating the odds. It's not about precise numbers, it’s about a gut feeling, a sense of how things are likely to play out. I definitely don’t see probability as a fixed scale. It’s more fluid, context-dependent. Two people saying the same thing can mean very different things depending on *how* they say it and what the situation is. Combining what people say…that’s tricky. You have to factor in who they are, what they’re trying to achieve, and how trustworthy they are. It’s a lot like reading a room, really.

profile_1713_gemma3:27b.thinking_process: Look, I’m a salesman. I deal with people all day, trying to convince them of things. Probability… it’s all about reading people, really. "Seguro" means you *know* something. “Casi seguro” means you’re 90% there, maybe you've got a good feeling, you’ve done your homework. “Probable” is like, a 60-70% chance. “Poco probable” – that’s a long shot, but not impossible. "Quizás" and "Puede ser" are… polite ways of saying you're not committing. Don't want to close the door completely, you understand? I use “Creo que sí” and “Pienso que sí” a lot when I'm trying to encourage someone, give them a little push. “Lo dudo” is when I’m being honest with myself, or with a trusted colleague, maybe. “Improbable” and “Muy improbable” I rarely say directly to a client, too negative. I'd rephrase that. "Igual al azar"… that’s like flipping a coin. “No sé” is for when I genuinely have no clue, but even then, I try to spin it positively. I use these words to subtly guide the conversation, to build trust, to get what I need. It’s not about cold calculation, it's about *feeling* the situation, knowing when to push and when to hold back. Combining them… that’s tricky. If someone says "quizás" and another says "quizás", you’re still not getting much, are you? Two positives, well, that reinforces things. But a strong positive and a weak one… you average it out, try to find a middle ground. It's all estimation, really. A man has to make a judgment call.

When I think about if something will happen, well, it depends. "Creo que sí" and "Pienso que sí" are pretty much the same to me, like, I have a feeling it will. Maybe 70, 80 percent. "Probable" is a little more… solid, more than just a feeling. "Improbable" and "Poco probable" are about the same, but "Muy improbable" is nearly impossible. "Casi seguro" is almost a guarantee, but people always say “almost.” Nothing’s *truly* certain. “Quizás” and “Puede ser” are just… maybe. 50/50. “Seguramente” is stronger than "Probable", but not as strong as “Seguro”. “Igual al azar” means it’s completely up to luck. “No sé” speaks for itself, doesn’t it? And “Lo dudo” is stronger than “No sé”, I’m leaning towards it *not* happening. "Difícilmente" is a polite way of saying “it won’t happen.”

When people say things together, well, you have to average it out, no? If two people both think it’s maybe, it’s still just a maybe. But if someone says it’s certain and someone says it’s very likely, you lean towards certain, of course. It's about finding a balance, a middle ground. I use these expressions all the time – with my family, with Ricardo’s clients… just everyday life.

If someone says "surely," they're pretty confident. If they say “I doubt it,” well, you know what that means. And if they say “I don't know”...that’s the truth, isn’t it? It's all gut feeling, really. It's not about numbers, it’s about experience. If someone asks me if a radio will work, I don’t give them a percentage. I tell them if I think it will, or if it’s a lost cause. Simple as that. Combining things… that's harder. If two people both say “maybe,” it *feels* like still just maybe, you know? I prefer to keep it vague enough to have some wiggle room. Combining opinions? That’s tricky. You gotta read the person, see if they’re an optimist or a pessimist. "Improbable" and "difícilmente," those are near enough to "no" that you almost give up. 

"Quizás" and “puede ser” – those are stalling words, really. Someone being polite, not wanting to say no directly. “Lo dudo” is a bit stronger, a definite leaning towards ‘no’, but still leaving a tiny door open. "Igual al azar"…that’s like flipping a coin, honestly. And "no sé," well, that’s just what it sounds like. 

When people give *numbers*, that’s a different story. They’re trying to be precise, but people aren't precise. Especially when it comes to things that haven't happened yet. It's all feeling, *intuición*, you understand? If two people both say something's likely, I’d add their percentages, but not directly. It’s not a simple math. If one says “seguro” and the other is vague, I’d trust the “seguro” more. You have to use your head, your experience. It’s about weighting what they say, not just taking it at face value.

Look, I sell insurance, right? So I’m *always* talking about probabilities. People don’t want to hear ‘no,’ they want to feel like there’s a chance, even if it’s small. “Creo que sí” and “Pienso que sí” – those are just polite ways of saying ‘yeah, probably’ when a client is asking if a policy covers something specific. "Seguro" is rarely used in everyday talk, feels too… absolute. Better to imply it. “Igual al azar” is just… a coin flip, really. "No sé" is for when I genuinely don't know, which isn't often, gotta be confident. “Lo dudo” is stronger than “Quizás,” more like a polite rejection. And “Difícilmente” – that’s a fancy way of saying "not likely," I'd use it with a client to try and upsell them to a better package.

When people give multiple answers, you have to find the middle ground, yeah? If someone says "seguro" and another says "seguramente," they basically mean the same thing. But if someone's totally unsure ("No sé") and another is somewhat confident ("Creo que sí"), you have to be careful. I always try to lean towards the more positive side, that’s just good salesmanship. Numbers are easier, obviously, but even then, you have to consider the person giving the number. Some people are naturally more pessimistic than others.

You’d be surprised if it *didn’t* happen. *Quizás* and *Puede ser*… those are just “maybe.” *Seguramente* is stronger than *Probable*, but not quite *Seguro*. *Igual al azar* means it’s a complete toss-up, like flipping a coin. *No sé* is just… I haven’t thought about it enough to have an opinion. *Lo dudo* is close to *Poco probable*, but a bit more personal, like you have a bad feeling. *Seguro* is definite, no question. *Difícilmente*… well, that's like saying it’s going to take a miracle.

When you put them together, it gets trickier. If someone says *Quizás* and someone else says *Quizás*, well, it’s still just “maybe.” Numbers are a bit easier to combine, but even then, it’s just a guess. If someone’s really sure and someone’s almost sure, I'd lean towards thinking it's pretty likely, but you never know. People can be wrong. Life is uncertain, you know? It depends on the situation, really.

profile_1817_gemma3:27b.thinking_process:
Look, I’ve been filing papers and answering phones at the municipal office for twenty years. You learn a thing or two about how people talk, how they avoid saying what they *really* think. Uncertainty… it's everywhere, isn’t it? Nobody wants to commit, especially not here in Argentina. We're used to things changing, plans falling through. So we soften everything with “quizás” and “puede ser”. It’s politeness, but it’s also… realism.

“Seguro” is rare, you know? Reserved for things like death and taxes. Even then, you might add a “toco de madera” just in case. "Probable" and "muy probable" are what I use when I want to be optimistic, maybe when talking about my son getting a good grade, but still keep a foot on the ground. “Improbable” and “muy improbable” – those are for the Boca Juniors winning the Libertadores three years in a row, that sort of thing.

The way people combine things... that’s interesting. If someone says “quizás” twice, it’s just a stronger “quizás”. Numbers are different. Two numbers, you add them up and split the difference, roughly. But if someone’s *sure* about something, well, that carries weight. It doesn’t matter what the other person says, really. You have to respect certainty, even if it's misplaced. I try to be careful with my words; a man’s word is all he has, after all. My abuela always said that.

profile_1898_gemma3:27b.thinking_process:
Ay, this is a bit strange, all these questions about *how* I think about things happening. Honestly, I don’t sit down and give numbers to whether something will happen or not. It’s just… a feeling, you know? I finished school, but I haven’t really *worked*, not a proper job. My husband, he works in construction, a good man, and we get by. I mostly look after the house, the kids when they were smaller… now they’re grown. So, I talk like everyone else, I guess.

“Creo que sí” – well, that means I think so, but it’s not a strong belief. It's like, *maybe* it will. “Improbable” is pretty clear, something’s not likely. “Probable” is the opposite, likely enough. “Poco probable” is weaker than improbable, something has a small chance. I use “Quizás” and “Puede ser” almost the same, like “maybe.” “Seguramente” is stronger, like I'm fairly confident. “Muy probable” is like, almost certain, but not quite. "Seguro" is certain.

When I say "Lo dudo," I'm doubtful. “Difícilmente” is similar to "poco probable" but maybe even a little weaker. “Igual al azar” means just a matter of luck. And “No sé”... well, that’s just what it sounds like, I don’t know!

If someone else gives me an idea of a chance, I kind of add it to what I already think. But it’s not a real calculation. I just try to get a feeling for what's most likely. My mother always said to trust your gut, and that’s what I do. It’s all a bit… abstract, isn’t it? I’m just a simple woman.

profile_1900_gemma3:27b.thinking_process:
Look, I've been working construction most of my life. Not a fancy job, but honest. You learn to gauge things, you know? When the foreman *says* something’s going to happen, that's pretty close to certain. But most of the time, it’s not like that. It’s always "maybe," "probably," "we'll see." It depends on the weather, if the materials arrive, if old man Rodriguez doesn't get a bad back.

These expressions… they're just common sense, really. "Seguro" is, well, sure. No doubt. "Poco probable" means it's a long shot, but not impossible. We say "quizás" all the time, just to be polite, even if we don't really think it will happen. It’s softer than saying “no”. “Lo dudo” is when you really have your doubts, like when my cousin promises to pay me back.

When people give you two opinions, you have to weigh them. If one person is a bit of a dreamer and the other is practical, you listen to the practical one more. You try to find the middle ground. Life isn't about certainties, it’s about assessing the chances and making a decision. You don’t want to be *too* optimistic, or you’ll get disappointed. But you also can’t be completely pessimistic, or you’ll never get anything done. It’s a balance. A little bit of hope, a little bit of realism. That’s how it is here in Argentina, anyway.

"Difícilmente" means it's possible, but not likely to happen on its own. My abuela used to say things like “casi seguro” a lot – close to certain, but she always had a little bit of doubt lingering. I don't think much about the exact *number* of probability, just the general feeling of it. When people give me two opinions I try to find a middle ground. If one person is very confident and the other isn’t, I tend to trust the confident one more. It’s just how things work around here, you know?

Honestly, this is a bit…academic. But interesting, I suppose. We use these phrases *constantly*. Who’s saying it? About what? A friend talking about winning the lottery? A doctor talking about a diagnosis? Completely different interpretations. 

I’d say I’m generally optimistic, so I tend to lean toward the higher side of probability when I’m unsure. But I also value being realistic. My parents raised me to be sensible, not a dreamer. We're a family of professionals – doctors, lawyers, architects – so precise language and considered judgments are important. When I'm discussing something serious, I want to convey a genuine assessment. But in casual conversation, it’s mostly just social lubrication. A little bit of hedging, a little bit of politeness. And “No sé” is *very* useful. I try not to pretend I know things I don't. Uncertainty is normal. 

When people give different opinions, you naturally try to average them out, subconsciously. You consider who's more informed, who’s more prone to exaggeration. It's all quite fluid. I'm trying to imagine how *I* would interpret these combinations – how it affects my own internal sense of possibility. It's tricky.

I suppose I use these expressions to be polite, to soften the blow sometimes, or to avoid committing fully when I’m not sure. You don’t want to seem arrogant, or to make promises you can't keep. With the combinations, you have to weigh it all up. Two maybes don’t suddenly become a certainty, do they? You have to see if they lean one way or the other. Numbers are more straightforward, naturally. A number is a number. But people… people are complicated. They use language to dance around the truth sometimes. I try to read between the lines, to get a sense of what they *really* mean.

profile_1967_gemma3:27b.thinking_process:
Honestly, these questions are a bit… academic. I’m a social worker, not a philosopher! But I can certainly think about how I use language every day. We deal with a lot of uncertainty in my line of work – people’s lives rarely go as planned. When I say "Creo que sí," or "Pienso que sí," it's generally when I have a feeling, an intuition, based on experience. It's not a guarantee, maybe 70-80% sure. "Probable" is a bit more solid, more than just a feeling; more like 60-70%. "Improbable" or "Poco probable" – those are for things that realistically aren’t going to happen, but you never say *never*… maybe 10-30%. "Muy improbable" is close to zero, practically a no. 

“Casi seguro” is strong, I’d put it around 90%. "Quizás" and “Puede ser” – those are genuinely up in the air, a real 50/50. "Seguramente" and "Seguro" are nearly the same, almost certain, like 95-100%. "Igual al azar"…that's a toss-up, like flipping a coin. "No sé" is obvious, complete uncertainty. "Lo dudo" is a polite way of saying I don't think so, maybe 20-30%. “Difícilmente” is similar to improbable, maybe a little stronger – a slight possibility, but low. 

When people give different opinions, you have to weigh things. Two "Quizás" – well, that confirms it's really uncertain. A high percentage plus another high percentage, you average them out. If someone's *sure* about something, that carries a lot of weight, but you still have to consider if they might be biased or misinformed. It’s all about trying to make the best judgment with incomplete information, isn’t it? We do that all the time.

"Perhaps" feels more formal, more distant. “I doubt it” is pretty direct, but even then, sometimes they're just negotiating tactics. With uncertainty, you don’t always get straight answers, eh? Especially not in Argentina. We like to soften things, be polite. So, you end up with a lot of hedging. I'd say I'm pretty good at interpreting those subtle differences. We all are, really, it’s just a matter of experience and paying attention. I wouldn’t overthink it too much; it's mostly gut feeling after a while. I use these expressions constantly, naturally. “Creo que sí” when I think things will go well, “poco probable” when I’m privately bracing for a setback. It’s how you navigate life, how you talk to people.

"Poco probable"… that’s when someone asks if I’ll win the lottery, basically. "Seguro" is… well, you're pretty sure. Like, the bus usually comes on time, *seguro*. “Quizás” and “Puede ser” are pretty much the same, a wishy-washy maybe. “Lo dudo” is when I really think something won't happen, but I don't want to be rude. It’s all about context, isn’t it? I just give my gut feeling. It’s not a science.

Look, I’ve been selling things – mostly household goods, you know, pots, pans, that sort of thing – for forty years now. You learn to read people, and a big part of that is how they *talk* about things being likely or not. It’s not always about the words themselves, but how they say them. "Seguro" is obvious, of course, but "creo que sí"… that’s a little softer. It means “I think so,” but it doesn’t commit you. You say that when you *hope* it's true, maybe, or when you’re trying to be polite. “Quizás” is just… well, it's up in the air. Could go either way. I use "puede ser" a lot when dealing with customers, trying to be agreeable but not promising anything I can't deliver. 

I don’t sit around thinking in percentages, though. It’s more of a feeling. Is it a good bet? Is it worth the risk? “Improbable” and “muy improbable” - those are clear enough. Something’s a long shot.  When I'm talking to my wife, I'll use "lo dudo" when she asks if I remembered to do something. It's a gentler way of saying "no," eh? Combining things... that’s trickier. If two people both say “quizás,” it still feels like a maybe, just a stronger one because two people think so. Numbers are too precise for real life, honestly. Everything’s a bit… fuzzy. I base it on how convincing the person sounds.

Ugh, another one of these things. My aunt, she’s always getting me to help with her student’s assignments, says it's good practice for my *pensamiento crítico*. Honestly, it feels like just… guessing. Like, how do you *put a number* on "maybe"? It depends on the situation, doesn't it? If someone asks if it will rain, "maybe" is different than if they ask if River Plate will win. 

I didn’t really finish my studies, you know? Had to help my mamá with my little brother after papá… well, nevermind. So I haven’t really had a proper job, just little things here and there to earn some pesos. So I don’t really think about probabilities in a scientific way, it's just what *feels* right. 

“Seguro” is obvious, one hundred percent, of course. “Improbable” feels pretty low, like almost zero. “Quizás”... that's like flipping a coin. "Puede ser" is a little more hopeful, like maybe there's a small chance. I use "creo que sí" a lot when my friend asks if I like a boy—I usually do, even if I'm shy.  

When people give you two opinions, it's hard. You have to try to find something in the middle, right? If someone's really sure, and someone else isn’t, you have to lean towards the sure one. It's like when you're trying to agree on a price at the *feria*—you can't just split the difference, you have to think about who needs to give a little more. It’s all just…common sense, I think.

profile_2006_gemma3:27b.thinking_process:
Look, I've spent my life working, first as a mechanic, now repairing appliances. Not a university professor, but my old man pushed for me to finish my studies – a *técnico* degree, good enough. It gives you a certain way of looking at things, of needing to be precise, but also knowing when you have to make a judgment call with what you've got. This business of how likely something is…it’s not about numbers for me, not really. It’s about feeling. “Creo que sí” – I mean, I think so, alright? Not a rock-solid certainty, but leaning that way. “Improbable”? That means I really don’t see it happening. "Seguro"…well, that's as close to a guarantee as you get in this life, and guarantees are rare. 

I use these phrases all the time, you know? Talking with my wife, with the neighbours, with customers. "Quizás" is my go-to when I don’t want to commit, or when something is genuinely up in the air. "Puede ser” is similar, maybe a little more resigned. When something feels nearly certain, like the heat will be unbearable in January, I say "casi seguro." If something is *really* unlikely, “difícilmente,” – it just doesn’t feel right. It's a subtle language. Combining them…that’s trickier. If two people both say “quizás,” it feels a bit stronger, but still vague. If someone gives me a number, a percentage, that makes it easier to pin down, but I still weigh it against my own gut feeling. People exaggerate, you know? They always do. I try to be realistic, to find a middle ground. It's about being sensible.

profile_2007_gemma3:27b.thinking_process:
Look, I'm a systems analyst – I deal with logic, with things needing to be *defined*. But life… life isn't defined, is it? Especially here. People talk, they hint, they say things vaguely. You learn to read between the lines, to understand what "perhaps" *really* means depending on who’s saying it, how they’re saying it, what they want from you. I'm not a philosopher, I just try to get by.

These expressions… they’re common sense, really. "Seguro" is obvious, "improbable" too. It’s the ones in the middle that are tricky. “Quizás” can mean anything from a 10% chance to 70%, depending on the context, the tone. “Puede ser” is even more slippery. I use "creo que sí" when I generally lean towards yes, but I don’t want to commit. It's polite. "Poco probable" I’d use when something is unlikely but not entirely impossible – like winning the lottery, maybe.

Combining things... that's what I do at work, so it’s a bit more natural. Two "quizás" together? It doesn't strengthen the likelihood, it just confirms the uncertainty. Numbers, though… people think they're precise, but they're just opinions disguised as facts. I try to average them, but I also consider who’s giving the number. Is it my *abuela*, or a statistician? It makes a difference. It all comes down to judgement, really. And a little bit of trying to figure out what someone *really* means.
